typhoon: Inspect and repair roof leaks, loose shingles, and weak windows
Trim tree branches that could fall on your house or power lines
Clean gutters and street drains to prevent localized flooding
Prepare a 3-day emergency go-bag (food, water, meds, flashlight, radio)
Establish a family communication plan and identify the nearest evacuation center
flood: Elevate heavy appliances and furniture off the ground floor
Place important documents (IDs, titles, birth certificates) in sealed waterproof bags
Identify an evacuation route to higher ground that avoids easily flooded streets
Install check valves in plumbing to prevent floodwater from backing up into drains
Check local hazard maps to understand your specific flood risk level
earthquake: Secure heavy furniture, cabinets, and appliances to the wall studs
Move heavy objects to lower shelves to prevent them from falling
Identify 'safe spots' in each room (e.g., under sturdy tables) and practice Drop/Cover/Hold On
Install latches on kitchen cabinets so plates and glass don't spill out
Locate your main electrical, gas, and water shut-off valves and learn how to turn them off
landslide: Check local hazard maps (via DENR-MGB) to see if your home is in a landslide-prone area
Plant ground-cover vegetation on slopes near your home to prevent soil erosion
Identify warning signs around your property (cracking ground, tilted trees, or shifting fences)
Create a preemptive evacuation plan for when heavy, continuous rain is forecasted
Ensure drainage paths on your property direct water away from steep slopes
fire: Install smoke alarms in key areas (kitchen, bedrooms) and test batteries monthly
Purchase a fire extinguisher for the kitchen and learn the PASS method (Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep)
Create a family escape plan identifying two ways out of every single room
Declutter combustible materials (newspapers, rags, cardboard) from near heat sources
Inspect home electrical wiring and replace any frayed cords or overloaded extension cables
volcanic_eruption: Stockpile N95 masks and protective goggles to guard against future ashfall
Purchase plastic sheeting and duct tape to seal windows and doors from ash
Check PHIVOLCS hazard maps to confirm your distance from the permanent danger zone
Include respiratory medications (like asthma inhalers) in your emergency go-bag
Plan an evacuation route that avoids river valleys prone to lahar flows
storm_surge: Consult PAGASA hazard maps to verify if your coastal area is vulnerable to storm surges
Map out a fast evacuation route to a location at least 15 meters above sea level
Prepare materials (sandbags, plywood) to board up coastal-facing windows and doors
Pack a go-bag and keep it by the door for rapid deployment
Plan where to safely relocate pets and livestock away from the shoreline
drought: Install rainwater catchment barrels or large water drums for non-potable use
Fix all leaky faucets, toilets, and pipes to conserve current water supplies
Stockpile commercially bottled drinking water (at least 1 gallon per person, per day)
Transition gardens to drought-resistant landscaping to minimize watering needs
Monitor NDRRMC and local water district advisories for rationing schedules
heat_wave: Service air conditioning units and clean electric fans to ensure they work efficiently
Install blackout curtains or window reflectors to keep the sun's heat out of the house
Stock up on oral rehydration salts (Oresol) and electrolyte drinks
Identify the coolest room in the house and set it up as a family retreat area
Learn the early medical signs of heat exhaustion and heat stroke
tsunami: Identify the highest inland point near your home or workplace
Physically walk your tsunami evacuation route to ensure it takes less than 15 minutes
Participate in community tsunami drills to familiarize yourself with the local warning sirens
Keep a grab-and-go bag easily accessible near the main exit of your home
Memorize the natural warning signs (strong earthquake, sudden receding of ocean water)
